text: The surgeon carefully manipulates the loop of the sigmoid colon, unfolding it from its deep position within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon carefully unfolds the loop of the sigmoid colon from deep within the abdominal cavity, using robotic instruments to manipulate the tissue.

The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection at the sacral promontory, creating a window beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery while considering the presence of mesh from a previous sacral colpopexy.

The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection at the sacral promontory, creating a window beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery while considering the presence of mesh from a previous sacral copephexy.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, carefully taking the vessel while navigating around the mesh from a previous sacral colpopexy.
The surgeon takes the vessel and performs a medial to lateral dissection, navigating around the mesh from a previous sacral copephexy.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field, likely identified during the dissection process.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a right-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, carefully maneuvering around the mesh from a previous sacral colpopexy.
The surgeon performs a right-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, carefully maneuvering around the visible mesh from a previous sacral colpopexy.
The surgeon uses two left hands for manipulation while the camera is positioned in arm three, providing a limited view of the right side of the rectum where mesh is present.
The surgeon operates with the camera positioned in arm three, providing a limited view of the right side of the rectum due to the presence of mesh.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection around the rectum, maneuvering carefully to navigate the mesh placement during the procedure.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection on the right side of the rectum, navigating around the mesh during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of a sponge to protect the ureter during dissection, ensuring safe manipulation of surrounding tissues without risk of injury.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of a sponge to protect the ureter, ensuring safe dissection above it without risk of injury, particularly in the absence of ICG visualization.
The surgeon encounters areas of thickened and woody bowel tissue, characteristic of diverticular disease, during the procedure.
The surgeon observes areas of the bowel affected by diverticular disease, noting the thick and woody appearance of the tissue.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection and proceeds to perform pelvic dissection, focusing on the anatomical structures within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection and proceeds to perform pelvic dissection, focusing on the lower abdominal region.
The surgeon performs a left-sided dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, carefully separating tissue layers in the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs a left-sided dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, carefully navigating the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon identifies the distal tattoo located just above the peritoneal reflection, approximately three centimeters distal to the tumor, indicating the area for further dissection below this mark.
The surgeon identifies the distal tattoo located just above the peritoneal reflection, approximately three centimeters distal to the tumor, indicating the area to dissect below.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum, carefully separating the tissue planes in this area.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum, carefully separating tissue layers in this area.
The surgeon observes that the mesh has not integrated into the anterior rectum, indicating a clear separation between the mesh and the rectal tissue.
The surgeon selects the distal margin and uses a vessel sealer to clear the mesentery, ensuring precise separation of the tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon selects the distal margin and uses a vessel sealer to clear the mesentery, ensuring precise separation of the tissue in preparation for resection.
The surgeon confirms that the mesh is no longer obstructing the surgical field and plans to perform the anastomosis just below the level where the mesh was previously located.
The surgeon completes the clearance of the rectal mesentery using a vessel sealer, ensuring the mesentery is fully separated from the rectum.
The surgeon completes the clearance of the rectal mesentery using a vessel sealer, ensuring the mesentery is fully separated from the rectal tissue.
The surgeon proceeds to take the proximal mesentery, starting from beneath the transection of the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon proceeds to take the proximal mesentery, beginning just beneath the transection of the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to preserve the left colic artery during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to preserve the left colic artery during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to staple the bowel after completing the dissection and removal of the mesentery.
The surgeon prepares to staple the bowel after taking the mesentery, positioning the stapler across the transected section.
The surgeon begins performing a hand-sewn two-layer anastomosis, meticulously stitching the bowel ends together.
The surgeon begins performing a hand-sewn two-layer anastomosis, meticulously stitching the bowel ends together.

The surgeon operates at or just below the level of the peritoneal reflection, using two right hands and one left hand for better visualization. The camera has been moved from port three to port two, and the surgeon joins the two posterior staple lines.
The surgeon operates at or just below the level of the peritoneal reflection, using two right hands and one left hand for improved visualization. The posterior staple lines are being joined.
The surgeon proceeds to make a proctotomy, creating an incision in the rectal tissue as part of the low anterior resection procedure.
The surgeon proceeds to make a proctotomy, creating an incision in the rectum as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins suturing the inner row, starting at the midline and joining the two posterior lips, progressing towards the patient's left side.
The surgeon begins suturing the inner row at the midline, joining the two posterior lips and extending the sutures towards the patient's left side.
The surgeon temporarily parks the suture and begins suturing in the midline, moving towards the patient's right side.
The surgeon temporarily parks the suture and begins suturing in the midline, moving towards the patient's right side.
The surgeon performs a canalization maneuver over the top of the surgical site, moving from the patient's right to left.
The surgeon performs a canalization maneuver over the top of the surgical site, moving from the patient's right to left.
The surgeon adjusts the left-sided suture, bringing it towards the midline to avoid convergence at a corner.
The surgeon adjusts the left-sided suture, bringing it towards the midline to avoid having the sutures meet at a corner.
The surgeon adjusts the left-sided suture towards the midline for a more natural alignment, then sutures from right to left to completely close the common channel.
The surgeon brings the suture towards the midline and proceeds to suture from right to left, effectively closing the common channel.
The surgeon ties the two sutures together at the midline, completing the closure of the common channel.
The surgeon ties the two sutures together at the midline, completing the closure of the common channel.
The surgeon uses the original suture to imbricate over the top of the two posterior staple lines, ensuring the canal is securely covered.
The surgeon takes the original suture that adjoined the two posterior staple lines and uses it to imbricate over the top, creating a reinforced closure.
The surgeon uses a nine-inch V-lock suture for the outer layer and six-inch V-lock sutures for the inner layers, demonstrating the selection of suture materials for the procedure.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis site, checking for any visible signs of leakage or fluid escape from the surgical connection.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the resected bowel segment to ensure there are no leaks at the anastomosis site.
